content:
# Acts Chapter 24 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 255-256 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_024_page_001.jpg, ACT_024_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And after five days the high priest Ananias came down with some elders and a certain orator, Tertullus, and they brought their case against Paul before the governor. **2** And when he had been summoned, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying,

"Since through you we enjoy much peace, and since reforms are being brought about for this nation by your foresight, **3** in every way and in every place we accept this, most excellent Felix, with all thankfulness. **4** But that I may not further detain you, I beg you in your forbearance to hear us briefly.

**5** "For we have found this man a pestilence, and one who stirs up dissension among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes, **6** who also attempted to profane the temple; and we seized him. **7** [omitted] **8** From whom you yourself, by examining him, will be able to learn about all these things of which we accuse him." **9** And the Jews also joined in the attack, affirming that these things were so.

**10** And when the governor had nodded to him to speak, Paul answered, "Knowing that for many years you have been a judge to this nation, I cheerfully make my defense concerning the things about myself, **11** as you are able to ascertain that it is not more than twelve days since I went up to worship in Jerusalem, **12** and neither in the temple did they find me disputing with anyone or stirring up a crowd, nor in the synagogues, nor throughout the city; **13** nor are they able to prove to you the things of which they now accuse me.

**14** "But this I confess to you, that according to the Way, which they call a sect, so I serve the God of our fathers, believing all things that are in accordance with the Law and that are written in the Prophets, **15** having a hope in God, which these men themselves also await, that there is going to be a resurrection of both the righteous and the unrighteous. **16** In this I also exercise myself to have a blameless conscience toward God and toward men always.

**17** "Now after many years I came to bring alms to my nation and offerings, **18** in the midst of which they found me purified in the temple, not with a crowd nor with a tumult; but there were certain Jews from Asia, **19** who ought to be present before you and to bring accusation, if they should have anything against me. **20** Or let these men themselves say what wrongdoing they found when I stood before the council, **21** unless it be concerning this one statement which I cried out standing among them: 'Concerning the resurrection of the dead I am being judged before you this day.'"

**22** But Felix, having a more accurate knowledge of the things concerning the Way, put them off, saying, "When Lysias the commander comes down, I will decide your case." **23** And he gave orders to the centurion that he should be kept in custody, and to have some liberty, and to prevent none of his own people from attending to him.

**24** And after some days, Felix came with Drusilla his own wife, who was a Jewess, and sent for Paul and heard him concerning the faith in Christ Jesus. **25** And as he reasoned about righteousness and self-control and the judgment that is to come, Felix became afraid and answered, "For the present, go away; and when I find an opportune time, I will summon you." **26** At the same time he was also hoping that money would be given to him by Paul; therefore he also sent for him more frequently and conversed with him.

**27** But when two years had been completed, Felix received a successor, Porcius Festus; and wishing to grant a favor to the Jews, Felix left Paul bound.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **v.1 — "orator" (ρητορος):** The Greek ρητωρ denotes a professional public speaker or advocate, functioning here as a legal attorney. Rendered "orator" to preserve the formal Greek sense; "lawyer" or "advocate" would convey the functional role but lose the classical nuance.

2. **v.5 — "pestilence" (λοιμον):** Sinaiticus reads λοιμον, a strong term of abuse meaning "plague" or "pestilent fellow." This is forensic rhetoric by Tertullus, characterizing Paul as a social contagion. Rendered literally.

3. **v.5 — "sect of the Nazarenes" (της των Ναζωραιων αιρεσεως):** The term αιρεσις here carries no inherently negative connotation in its original Greek usage (meaning "party" or "school of thought"), though Tertullus employs it pejoratively. "Sect" preserves the ambiguity present in the Greek.

4. **vv.6b-8a — Omitted Western text:** Sinaiticus omits the longer Western reading found in some manuscripts (vv.6b-8a), which includes "and we would have judged him according to our law, but the commander Lysias came and with great violence took him from our hands, commanding his accusers to come before you." The verse numbering reflects this omission; v.7 is not present in this manuscript.

5. **v.14 — "the Way" (την οδον):** Paul's self-designation for the Christian movement, used consistently in Acts (9:2; 19:9, 23; 22:4; 24:22). Rendered as a proper noun to reflect its technical usage as a recognized designation for early Christianity.

6. **v.15 — "of both the righteous and the unrighteous":** Sinaiticus includes the full phrase δικαιων τε και αδικων, affirming a universal resurrection — a characteristically Pharisaic belief that Paul claims continuity with.

7. **v.22 — "a more accurate knowledge" (ακριβεστερον ειδως):** The comparative form suggests Felix already possessed some familiarity with Christianity, not that he gained it from the trial proceedings. This aligns with his marriage to the Jewish Drusilla (v.24).

8. **v.24 — "Drusilla his own wife" (Δρουσιλλη τη ιδια γυναικι):** Sinaiticus includes ιδια ("own"), emphasizing the marital relationship. Drusilla was the daughter of Herod Agrippa I and sister of Agrippa II, historically known to have left her former husband to marry Felix.

9. **v.25 — "righteousness and self-control and the judgment":** Paul's three topics — δικαιοσυνη, εγκρατεια, and κριμα — are pointedly relevant to Felix's personal life, explaining his fearful reaction. The formal translation preserves each term distinctly.

10. **v.27 — "wishing to grant a favor" (θελων χαριτα καταθεσθαι):** Sinaiticus reads χαριτα (accusative of χαρις). Felix's political motivation for leaving Paul imprisoned is made explicit by Luke. The phrase καταθεσθαι χαριτα is an idiomatic expression meaning "to deposit a favor" — rendered naturally as "to grant a favor."

— LOGOS
